item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As a Korean, I feel this on a spiritual level. +0.99

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: She only erases half of 682

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why personnel won't order to her "erase SCP-682 from reality".
I really enjoyed this SCP, was super interesting and a fun read, especially the test logs, makes me want more to see her true capabilities.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm guessing the designation itself being 2599 instead of 2600 is a reference to her being 'not good enough'?

i've got executive dysfunction and asian parents, so this one hit me like a one-two punch in the gut. i love skips that are the encapsulation/embodiment of a particular human feeling, and this might be my favorite example. +1

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Damn this is a really good article. Apparently several others think so, as SCP-2599 is refrenced frequently in other articles, including SCP-894, SCP-2160, and the SCP-682 Log. All of these articles contain additional crosslinks, and so linking from here to there not only completes the loop of these articles, but facilitates additional wikiwalking. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

The following is a testing log concerning SCP-2599. Personnel conducting tests with SCP-2599 should consult Dr. Wensley before appending this log.

To this:

The following is a testing log concerning SCP-2599. Personnel conducting tests with SCP-2599 should consult Dr. Wensley before appending this log. Use of SCP-894 by personnel and D-class has been approved to prevent unwanted activation of SCP-2599's effect. Testing of SCP-2599 with additional anomalies is approved on a case by case basis.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fan theory: Mr. Deeds was asked by a woman to have sex for the sake of reproduction (i.e. having a biological child, as opposed to adoption), and he did what he need to do. The result was that one-night stand was 2599, who managed to have a slitter of Mr. Deeds' power but diluted by 2599's human half.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder if she is related to Mr. Deeds?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoy this.

To enjoy myself even more, I would like to break down the following "paradox" with a deductive(?) methodology for everyone:

'The following sentence is true. The previous sentence is false.'

"The previous sentence is false" is true.
Which means
"The following sentence is true" is false.
Abiding by this rule, then
"The previous sentence is false" can not be true.
Which means that, since it it can't be true that the below quote is (explicitly) false, then
"The following sentence is true" can not be "false", but the problem arise when the paradox gives you literal options.

Still with me? Great. Now, since
"The following sentence is true" can not be "false", it can by anything but false. It can be "kind of true", for instance.
Using the above example, assuming it is "kind of true", then
"The previous sentence is false" is probably mostly true.
So if above quote probably is mostly true, then
"The following sentence is true" should probably be mostly false but it may also actually be somewhat true. You can't know, really.. you start lacking "proof". It's just (deductively) more likely to be false than true..
So if (…) then…
Etc. etc.

And everything starts blurring.

Hope anyone potentially reading this enjoyed it as much as I did typing it ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. A lot. I can't explain much more than that; you just did good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it's the "too much like a specific request" one- an open-ended suggestion like "Would you like to go for a walk?" wouldn't have any specific conditions for completion or implied obligation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a little confused. The containment procedures state that suggestions aren't susceptible to either of her effects, yet in the interview, she can't complete a suggested task. Is it just that it wasn’t suggestiony enough? Or did I miss something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man, when I first read the premise, my head is filled with dark situation of how she might be exploited before the Foundation take her in. It's good that what happen isn't as bad.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
<TheNoah> Doctor: "2599?" 2599:"yes?" Doctor: "End the universe please." 2599:"Okay" *universe is halfway destroyed

^ Would have been an amazing zinger.

The dialogue is extremely awkward. Tone isn't the best. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me of a character from Red vs Blue: highly capable but will fail at the end of doing anything important.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting concept! The article didn't make me want to skip a few paragraphs to get to the interesting stuff. You did a good job! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I LOVE this SCP.
When I started reading it, I expected it to just be another Human What Alters Reality with its Minds, but you ju-
Forget it, UPVOTED.

EDIT: Re-read this one today. Downvoted, then upvoted again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is a perfect example of doing an over used idea but in a way that works and has a new twist to it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Weizhong, you- I was gonna call you something bad.

It might be because of my personal feelings on the matter, but upvoted. A lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I respectfully disagree. She periodically has the portions of her memory involving her time with the Foundation removed. After that, you would expect her to ask that her real name be used and when she can be going home and other things like that.

I think this is well written, and an interesting take on reality benders. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Edited slightly to remove t-the e-effect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The researchers shot the D-Class first.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree. A very good read. The test logs sealed it for me. I understand why testing was stopped, but I can't keep from imagining different ones in my head. Fun concept for a reality bender.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding this and adding in that wr-writing like th-this d-doesn't have the e-effect you th-ink it d-does.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is no critique that someone can't dismissively refer to as useless. None. The only determining factor is whether the person WANTS to ignore criticizing the SCP itself and focus entirely on criticizing someone who took the time out of their day to read something and took the time to politely respond to the parts of it that disappointed them. The "worst" and "useless" metacriticisms applied to legitimately phrased criticisms, without a doubt, constitute unnecessary backbiting and borderline backseat moderating, since the wiki (not "the SCP") already has people whose job it is to police criticisms for violations of the Criticism Policy. This critique says nothing whatsoever to the author of the piece whose discussion page we're on, and very often convinces critics that they should keep their mouth shut for fear of taking heat from people like you, who neither wrote the article nor actually provided any critique, positive or negative, for the benefit of anybody.

Seriously, comment on articles, not comments. Leave moderation to the moderators.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is no object that someone couldn't dismissively refer to as a magic item. None. The only determining factor is whether the person WANTS to dismissively refer to it as a magic item. The "magic item" and "cursed magic item" criticisms are without a doubt, the worst, most useless ideas the SCP seems to think are insightful and valuable. "Magic item" conveys no information whatsoever to the author, has no relevance to anything in the article, and very often convinces readers that an acceptable article is terrible, or a terrible article's problems stem from the fact that it's a "magic item" and not the thing that's really wrong with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Cliché, I know, but why do I have the feeling that her power will go away if someone says "you did a good job" to her?

Anyway, have an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Her powers weren't interesting until the test logs. (Though I'm confused by the one that involved a D-class being shot in the head. She she manifest the bullet, or did one of the researchers shoot him?)

That said, the interview is way clunky, for reasons stated above. The final addendum is pretty all right, but overall, not enough for an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was with you up until the ridiculously hamfisted interview. It's already a Special Teenage Girl What's in Confinement, we absolutely do not need it to ask to be called by name and then get the sads.

I would upvote this without the interview log and lolrandom incident at the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Giving a magic item drawbacks just makes it a cursed magic item.

Giving a magic human drawbacks just makes it a cursed magic human.

The deliberate attempt to tug the heartstrings did not distract from the above interpretation for me. Possibly because the tugging wasn't particularly effective or original in my opinion.

Just don't buy it, sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this SCP. It's well-written, and the conversation log grabs your heart while still being within the scope of what feels "Foundation-like." The effects, while seeming simple at first, have been demonstrated to have a wide variety of results in testing.

It's good. It just is. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 3sad5me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Random note I got the Manchurian Candidate vibe from this article

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was well written and a good way to subtly present a dangerous reality bender.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Given Command: SCP-2599 is ordered to be happier.
Outcome: SCP-2599 experiences enhanced dopamine and serotonin levels for approximately 10 seconds before they become lower than the original level they started at.

Zyn crey

Oh man. I know the feeling. I feel for this SCP. I has so many feels. And the title perfectly captures it.

Good work. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As I said in the chat, I really like this as a reality bender.
The test log is entertaining and the interview is enjoyable to read. +1

item.title: Author Overview
item.content:encoded:
This was inspired by a joke that I had with my friends that my parents' standards were always slightly above what I actually can do. Somehow, this got spun off into a full on SCP.

Somehow.

Anyways, thanks to the assorted members of chat for making this happen, but Devereaux, JConwell, Bryx, TwistedGears, and a few others as well in particular.
